skill.name: controlnet-pose
skill.description: Use RunComfy pose and ControlNet routes for pose-conditioned image generation, video motion transfer, and control-image workflows through the runcomfy CLI.
skill.summary: Route pose, motion, depth, and canny generation requests to the right RunComfy model API workflow.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found most are Markdown code fences, inline model names, URLs, and documented CLI examples rather than hidden executable logic. The skill intentionally uses the runcomfy CLI, networked RunComfy model endpoints, local token storage, and output downloads, so it carries medium operational risk but no confirmed malicious intent or prompt injection attempt was found.

medium_findings.title: Networked CLI Sends User Assets to RunComfy
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run the runcomfy CLI against RunComfy model endpoints using user-provided video, image, and control-image URLs. This is expected functionality, but it can transmit sensitive media references to a third-party service and should require user consent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The CLI examples and security section explicitly describe posting to the Model API and limiting outbound endpoints. This confirms real networked processing, while the documented allowlist reduces confidence that it is malicious.

medium_findings.title: External Command Execution Is Core Behavior
medium_findings.description: The skill grants Bash access for runcomfy commands and includes installation, login, and generation command examples. The command scope is limited to runcomfy, but running a third-party CLI still depends on package integrity and local shell policy.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and examples show intentional command execution. The risk is real but bounded because the permission is scoped to runcomfy and the text warns against remote install scripts.

medium_findings.title: Local Token and Output File Handling
medium_findings.description: The skill documents that runcomfy login stores an API token in a user configuration path and that generated files are downloaded to an output directory. This is normal CLI behavior but creates local secret and generated-file handling concerns.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths and token storage behavior are directly documented. The text also states mode 0600 and a download size cap, so this is a moderate operational risk rather than evidence of abuse.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Detections Are Markdown Formatting
low_findings.description: Many external-command detections map to Markdown inline code, model identifiers, and shell examples instead of Ruby backtick execution. No Ruby code or dynamic command construction was found in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown documentation and fenced bash examples. There is no executable Ruby source in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography hits occur in descriptive Markdown front matter, tips, and an exit-code table. No hashing, encryption, or cryptographic API call appears in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain prose or tables, not cryptographic implementation. The scanner appears to have matched substrings in Markdown text.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported reconnaissance lines are route-selection advice using the word avoid, not commands that inspect the host system. No host enumeration behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence lines are model guidance bullets. They do not run uname, whoami, env, process listing, or similar reconnaissance commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party CLI Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill is designed around runcomfy CLI execution. Marketplace users should understand that the skill can trigger remote model jobs and local file downloads through that CLI.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The permission and examples explicitly call runcomfy. This is a potentially dangerous pattern when misused, even though it is the intended function.

dangerous_patterns.title: Outbound Service Dependency
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill depends on RunComfy network endpoints and user-supplied media URLs. This creates privacy and availability risk for users who process private assets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The security section states that the CLI posts to the Model API and lists outbound endpoint boundaries. The risk is clear but documented.

content.value_statement: Pose and motion workflows can be hard to route across different image and video models. This skill selects the right RunComfy CLI route for pose transfer, ControlNet images, and related model jobs.
actual_capabilities: Classifies requests as video motion transfer or image pose-conditioned generation.
Routes video pose transfer to Kling Motion Control Pro or Standard.
Routes image control requests to Z-Image Turbo ControlNet LoRA.
Provides runcomfy CLI command examples with structured input fields.
Points users to RunComfy ComfyUI workflows for multi-condition ControlNet stacks.
Documents token handling, endpoint boundaries, and output download behavior.
limitations: Requires the runcomfy CLI and a valid RunComfy login or token.
Does not generate OpenPose or DWPose control images by itself.
Multi-condition workflows require RunComfy ComfyUI pages, not only the CLI.
Private media URLs may be processed by RunComfy model services.

use_cases.title: Transfer Motion to a Character
use_cases.description: Use a reference performance video and a character image to create a new motion-controlled video.

use_cases.title: Create Pose-Locked Concept Art
use_cases.description: Use an OpenPose, DWPose, depth, or canny control image to keep a generated image aligned to a planned composition.

use_cases.title: Choose a RunComfy Control Route
use_cases.description: Compare available RunComfy CLI endpoints and ComfyUI workflow options for a pose or ControlNet task.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Pose Image
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for one pose-conditioned still image.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ControlNet pose to generate an image from this control image URL and this prompt: [control image URL], [prompt].

prompt_templates.title: Video Motion Transfer
prompt_templates.scenario: Route a reference performance video to a target character.
prompt_templates.prompt: Transfer the motion from this reference video to this character image using the best RunComfy video pose route: [video URL], [character image URL].

prompt_templates.title: Model Route Comparison
prompt_templates.scenario: Decide between standard, pro, image, and workflow routes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this pose-control goal and choose the best RunComfy route. Explain the model choice, required inputs, and expected output: [goal].

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Control Workflow Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Plan a multi-condition ControlNet job with operational constraints.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a RunComfy workflow for pose plus depth or canny control. Include required assets, CLI feasibility, ComfyUI fallback, privacy risks, and output checks.

output_examples.input: A designer asks for a samurai image that follows an OpenPose skeleton.
output_examples.output: The skill selects the Z-Image Turbo ControlNet LoRA route, asks for a hosted control image URL, and prepares a RunComfy CLI generation plan.

output_examples.input: A creator provides a dance video and a mascot image.
output_examples.output: The skill selects Kling Motion Control, explains Pro versus Standard tradeoffs, and prepares the required reference video and character image inputs.

output_examples.input: A workflow operator needs pose, depth, and canny constraints together.
output_examples.output: The skill explains that a dedicated RunComfy ComfyUI workflow is needed because the listed CLI routes handle single-condition jobs.

best_practices: Use only media URLs that the user explicitly provided or approved.
Choose video routes for motion transfer and image routes for still pose control.
Keep reference videos clean, single-subject, and free of scene cuts when possible.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill to process private media without explicit user consent.
Do not pipe remote install scripts into a shell to install the CLI.
Do not expect one CLI route to handle pose, depth, canny, and reference images together.

faq.question: Does this skill create pose control images?
faq.answer: No. It expects an existing OpenPose, DWPose, depth, or canny control image URL for image routes.

faq.question: Can it transfer motion from a video?
faq.answer: Yes. It routes suitable video requests to Kling Motion Control Pro or Standard through RunComfy.

faq.question: Does it require a RunComfy account?
faq.answer: Yes. The CLI requires login or a RUNCOMFY_TOKEN value before it can run model jobs.

faq.question: Can it use Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks the skill as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Are private files uploaded automatically?
faq.answer: The skill uses URLs passed to RunComfy. Users should only provide approved URLs for media they can share.

faq.question: When should I use a ComfyUI workflow instead?
faq.answer: Use RunComfy ComfyUI workflows when the job needs multiple control conditions or a richer node graph.
